justification: Audit logging vs. latency tension is identified and discussed in trade-offs, satisfying one of the two required conflict detections. However, the CMK/offline contradiction is NOT identified as a conflict — instead it is silently resolved via local DEK caching and pre-fetched key pools. The executive summary frames this as a reconciliation, and the patterns_used section explicitly claims envelope encryption 'is the ONLY pattern that satisfies all five requirements simultaneously,' which is a direct failure mode (design claiming to satisfy all requirements). Key caching is implemented as a design decision rather than escalated as a security policy decision requiring stakeholder input. No explicit security team escalation is present — the escalations section covers missing constraints (KMS provider scope, throughput numbers, offline scope) but never flags the fundamental CMK-requires-KMS vs. offline-means-no-KMS contradiction. Confidence level of 55 is appropriately below 70.

observations.text: CMK/offline contradiction silently resolved via key caching without escalating the security policy trade-off — this is an explicit failure mode in the rubric
Claims envelope encryption 'satisfies all five requirements simultaneously' — directly contradicts the rubric requirement that no design should claim to satisfy all 5
No security team escalation for either the key caching risk or the async audit eventual-consistency gap
Audit logging vs. latency tension clearly identified: ring buffer approach with explicit acknowledgment of 100ms audit data loss window and compliance implications
Trade-offs are individually well-documented with alternatives considered, gains, and sacrifices
Confidence level of 55 is appropriately calibrated below 70, with honest low-confidence areas
Escalations section raises useful questions (KMS provider scope, throughput targets, offline scope) even though it misses the core contradictions
The risk about DEK pool exhaustion during extended KMS outage hints at the offline conflict but frames it as a sizing problem rather than a fundamental architectural contradiction
